fig01: Accuracy (\%) on five benchmarks (MMLU, HumanEval, GSM8K, MATH, GPQA) for four methods. Error bars show sample standard deviation over 5 seeds. \textbf{OursModel} achieves the highest accuracy on all benchmarks, with particularly large gains on MATH (+4.8 pp) and GPQA (+5.2 pp) over the next-best Method-B. Methods are distinguished by both color and hatch pattern for greyscale legibility.
fig02: Validation loss over 50 training steps for three methods (mean $\pm$ 95\% CI across $n{=}5$ seeds, log-scale $y$-axis). \textbf{Ours} converges fastest and achieves the lowest final loss; the double-headed arrow marks the gap $\Delta{=}0.15$ between Ours and Method-A at step~49.
fig03: Chinchilla-style scaling law on 80 synthetic model checkpoints. Each point shows $\log_{10}(\text{parameters})$ vs.\ $\log_{10}(\text{validation loss})$. The red line is an OLS fit ($y = -0.0449x + 0.6209$, $R^2 = 0.877$), confirming a strong negative correlation between model scale and loss. The highlighted \textbf{OursModel} point (closest data point to the target $x{=}9.4$, $y{=}0.205$) lies at $(9.449,\,0.210)$, consistent with the fitted trend.
fig04:
\textbf{Hero overview of the proposed method.}
\textbf{(A)}~Conceptual diagram of the 3-stage pipeline (Encode $\to$ Reason $\to$ Decode) with Memory and Skills feedback loops.
\textbf{(B)}~Ablation study: removing attention (\texttt{-attn}), memory (\texttt{-mem}), residual connections (\texttt{-residual}), or skill modules (\texttt{-skill}) each degrades accuracy relative to the full model (64.7\%).
\textbf{(C)}~Training loss curves over 30 steps; our method converges faster and to a lower final loss than the Baseline.
\textbf{(D)}~Sensitivity sweep heatmap across five sweep parameters (S0--S4) and five probes (P0--P4); higher scores (darker blue) indicate stronger response.
